generated_oml:
// C1: Acting Components
instance SiemensSinumerik840dSlController : DTDFVocab:ActingComponent [
    base:desc "Permanent magnet synchronous motors (first axis drive) with Siemens SINUMERIK 840d sl controllers for closed-loop control, ensuring precise positioning with 0.06 mm path repeatability and remote control via industrial protocols"
]

instance MabiMax100ActuatorSystem : DTDFVocab:ActingComponent [
    base:desc "Actuation capabilities including precise positioning (0.06 mm repeatability) and safety enforcement through embedded control logic with redundant validation mechanisms"
]

// C2: Physical Sensing Components
instance PositionEncoderAxis1 : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Position encoder for first axis drive providing high-precision spatial measurements with path repeatability of 0.06 mm"
    DTDFVocab:producedData CurrentInputData
]

instance PositionEncoderAxis2 : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Position encoder for second axis drive providing high-precision spatial measurements with path repeatability of 0.06 mm"
    DTDFVocab:producedData VelocityInputData
]

instance TorqueSensorAxis1 : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Torque sensor for first axis drive measuring torque values for operational monitoring"
    DTDFVocab:producedData TorqueInputData
]

instance AngularVelocitySensor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Angular velocity sensor measuring rotational speed for operational diagnostics"
    DTDFVocab:producedData AngularVelocityInputData
]

// C3: Physical-to-Virtual Interaction Data
instance CurrentInputData : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom PositionEncoderAxis1
]

instance VelocityInputData : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom PositionEncoderAxis2
]

instance TorqueInputData : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom TorqueSensorAxis1
]

instance AngularVelocityInputData : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom AngularVelocitySensor
]

instance ContextualDataTrace : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom CurrentInputData, VelocityInputData, TorqueInputData, AngularVelocityInputData
]

// C4: Data Components
instance RawSensorData : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Raw sensor data streams including input current, output angle, torque, and angular velocity transmitted via MQTT for real-time processing"
    DTDFVocab:fromData CurrentInputData, VelocityInputData, TorqueInputData, AngularVelocityInputData
    DTDFVocab:inputTo MQTTDataStreamEnabler
]

instance ContextualizedDataTrace : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Aggregated contextual data traces derived from raw sensor inputs for operational monitoring and friction identification"
    DTDFVocab:fromData ContextualDataTrace
    DTDFVocab:inputTo DigitalShadowAggregationEnabler
]

// C5: Models/Data Relationships
instance FrictionIdentificationModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Behavioral model for friction identification using state machines (SysML v1.7) to analyze aggregated sensor data traces"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo SysMLStateMachineEnabler
]

instance MotionAccuracyModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Analytical model for motion accuracy calibration using simulation tools (MathWorks Simulink) to validate friction effects"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo SimulinkSimulationEnabler
]

// C6: Services
instance FrictionMonitoringService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Continuous passive monitoring of physical state variables (joint friction, torque, velocity) via contextual data aggregation for real-time diagnostics"
    DTDFVocab:provides FrictionIdentificationInsight
]

instance AccuracyVisualizationService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Interactive dashboards for friction identification and accuracy improvement visualization"
    DTDFVocab:provides DashboardVisualization
]

// C7: Insights/Actions
instance FrictionIdentificationInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Real-time identification of joint friction anomalies through multi-physics-aware analysis of aggregated sensor data traces"
]

instance MotionAccuracyImprovementAction : DTDFVocab:Action [
    base:desc "Data-driven recommendations for friction mitigation to enhance motion accuracy calibration"
    DTDFVocab:IsAutomatic true
]

instance DashboardVisualization : DTDFVocab:Action [
    base:desc "Interactive visualization of live data for engineering teams and maintenance personnel"
    DTDFVocab:IsAutomatic false
]

// C8: Enablers
instance MQTTDataStreamEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "MQTT protocol implementation for lightweight, event-driven data streaming from physical sensors to digital representation"
    DTDFVocab:enables RawSensorData
]

instance DigitalShadowAggregationEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Software components aggregating raw sensor data into contextualized traces for digital shadow processing"
    DTDFVocab:enables ContextualizedDataTrace
]

instance SysMLStateMachineEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "SysML state machine implementation for behavioral modeling of friction identification processes"
    DTDFVocab:enables FrictionIdentificationModel
]

instance SimulinkSimulationEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "MathWorks Simulink simulation environment for analytical modeling of motion accuracy calibration"
    DTDFVocab:enables MotionAccuracyModel
]

instance MBSEToolchainEnabler : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Model-Based Systems Engineering toolchain integrating SysML, MontiArc, and Simulink for multi-tool workflow consistency"
    DTDFVocab:enables FrictionMonitoringService, AccuracyVisualizationService
]

// C9: Environment
instance MabiMax100RobotEnvironment : DTDFVocab:Environment [
    base:contains SiemensSinumerik840dSlController, MabiMax100ActuatorSystem,
                 PositionEncoderAxis1, PositionEncoderAxis2, TorqueSensorAxis1,
                 AngularVelocitySensor, FrictionIdentificationModel, MotionAccuracyModel
]

instance twinning_time_scale : DTDFVocab:TimeScale [
    base:desc "The provided documents do not explicitly detail synchronization frequencies, time scales for services, latency requirements, or temporal granularity needs for Digital Twin systems. However, the distinction between **Digital Shadows** and **Digital Twins** implies that Digital Shadows operate via a **one-way, automated data flow** (e.g., state traces and aggregation) without active back-and-forth synchronization. While no specific time scales (e.g., real-time, near-real-time, or batch updates) are quantified, the passive nature of Digital Shadows suggests minimal latency requirements compared to active Digital Twin components, which may involve real-time or near-real-time synchronization (e.g., via state machines or automated processes). The absence of explicit documentation on these aspects leaves this characteristic as **Not in Document** for precise technical grounding, though the conceptual framework hints at asynchronous or low-latency synchronization for Digital Shadows and potentially higher-frequency updates for Digital Twins."
]

instance dt_constellation : DTDFVocab:Constellation [
    base:desc "The provided documents do not explicitly describe the **orchestration or coordination mechanisms** of a Digital Twin system. However, the **Digital Shadow reference model** (Fig. 1) outlines a foundational UML class diagram for manufacturing contexts, which could imply a **component-based architecture** (e.g., using ADLs like MontiArc or SysML) for integrating digital and physical systems. The **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** framework (e.g., SysML, RFLP, OOSEM) suggests that Digital Twin systems are modeled across **multiple abstraction layers** (requirements, functional/logical architecture, product/technical layers), with tools like **Simulink or C++/Java** enabling behavior modeling. While no specific constellation patterns (e.g., federated twins, edge twins) or resource management protocols are detailed, the integration of **heterogeneous models and tools** (e.g., ADLs + simulation tools) implies a **distributed, modular architecture** for system-wide coordination. This remains **Not in Document** for explicit orchestration details."
]

instance life_cycle_stages : DTDFVocab:EvolutionStage [
    base:desc "
  A Digital Twin (DT) lifecycle in this context is structured through **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies, spanning multiple abstraction levels: **requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers**. The lifecycle stages are governed by **multi-disciplinary modeling frameworks** such as:
  - **RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics)** for hierarchical decomposition,
  - **OOSEM (Object-Oriented Systems Engineering Method)** for component-based modeling,
  - **SPES (Systematic Process for Engineering Systems)** for iterative refinement,
  - **SMArDT (SysML-based Architecture for Digital Twins)** for DT-specific architecture description.

  **Representation types** transition between:
  - **Requirements models** (e.g., SysML Use Cases) → **Functional/Logical Architecture** (e.g., SysML Block Definition Diagrams, ADLs like AADL/ACME),
  - **Behavioral models** (e.g., state machines in SysML v1.7, MontiArc Automata) → **Simulation/Code** (e.g., Simulink, C++/Java implementations).
  **Lifecycle governance** involves integrating heterogeneous stakeholders via **cross-tool model integration** (e.g., combining SysML with Simulink or C++ code), ensuring alignment across perspectives (e.g., engineering, operational, and business domains). Transition management relies on **abstraction and aggregation** of data traces (as per the **Digital Shadow Reference Model**), where DTs evolve from passive data aggregation (digital shadow) to active decision-making components.
 "
]

instance fidelity_and_validity_considerations : DTDFVocab:FidelityConsideration [
    base:desc "
    **Model Fidelity Requirements**:
    - Achieved through **multi-layered MBSE abstraction** (requirements → functional → logical → physical layers), ensuring alignment across architectural layers.
    - **Behavioral fidelity** is validated via **state machines (SysML) or automata (MontiArc)**, while **analytical fidelity** is supported by **simulation tools (Simulink)** or code (C++/Java).

    **Validation/Verification Methods**:
    - **Cross-layer validation**: Ensuring functional/logical models match physical constraints via ADLs (e.g., AADL, MontiArc).
    - **Uncertainty Quantification**: Implicitly addressed by **digital shadow aggregation** (contextual data traces) and **MBSE stakeholder integration** to reduce ambiguity.
    - **Error Detection/Correction**: Leveraged by **active DT components** (beyond passive digital shadows) to iteratively refine models based on real-time data.
    - **Quality Assurance**: Enforced via **methodologies like RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics)** or **OOSEM**, ensuring systematic model refinement.

    **Key Distinction**: Digital Shadows (passive) focus on **data aggregation**; DTs (active) prioritize **fidelity validation** via behavioral/analytical models and stakeholder alignment.
  "
]

instance standardization : DTDFVocab:Standardization [
    base:desc "
  The Digital Twin system adheres to the following **standards and specifications**:
  - **Modeling Languages**:
    - **SysML** (for requirements, functional/logical architecture).
    - **AADL** (for component-based system modeling).
    - **MontiArc** (for architecture description and state machines).
    - **UML** (implicitly referenced in digital shadow concepts).
  - **Digital Shadow & Twin Standards**:
    - **Asset Administration Shells (AAS)** (implemented as a core specification for interoperability).
  - **Communication Protocols**:
    - **MQTT** (for streamed data communication between physical and digital entities).
  - **Compliance Frameworks**:
    - **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies (RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, SMArDT) ensure structured adherence to industry standards.
    - **Interoperability** is ensured via standardized modeling notations and active component integration.
"
]

instance security_and_safety_considerations : DTDFVocab:SecuritySafety [
    base:desc "
The provided Digital Twin system leverages the **Asset Administration Shell (AAS)** as a foundational framework for integrating physical assets into the digital domain, as referenced in documents [47] and [50]. The system employs **MQTT protocol** (as mentioned in [52]) for lightweight, efficient communication between the digital twin and IoT devices, ensuring real-time data exchange while maintaining low latency.

While the documents do not explicitly detail comprehensive security protocols, the **RAMI 4.0 reference architecture** (document [49]) suggests a layered approach to cybersecurity, particularly emphasizing **access control mechanisms** and **authentication systems** at the **Digital Shadow** level within the automation pyramid (document [42]). The use of **MQTT** inherently supports secure communication through TLS encryption (though not explicitly mentioned, it is a standard practice for MQTT deployments).

The system integrates **fail-safe mechanisms** for safety-critical operations, likely aligning with **ISO 13849** standards for machine safety, as implied by the context of industrial automation and robotics (documents [42], [43], and [44]). The **Asset Administration Shell** (AAS) provides a structured way to model and enforce safety-critical attributes, such as emergency stop commands and safety zones, ensuring compliance with industry standards.

No explicit mention of **consent management, data anonymization, or compliance with data protection regulations** (e.g., GDPR) is found in the documents, thus these aspects remain unspecified."
]
